blocks.text: **Control flow** determines the order in which a program's statements execute. Without control flow, every program would just run top-to-bottom once and stop. With it, you can make decisions, repeat actions, and respond to different conditions.
Use conditionals to execute different code based on whether a condition is true or false.

blocks.title: if / elif / else in Python
if / else if / else in JavaScript
Comparison Operators
blocks.text: `==` (equal), `!=` (not equal), `>` (greater), `<` (less), `>=` (greater or equal), `<=` (less or equal). In JavaScript, prefer `===` (strict equality, checks type too) over `==` (loose equality, with type coercion).

blocks.title: Combining conditions
blocks.code:
age = 25
has_ticket = True
is_banned = False

# and — both must be True
if age >= 18 and has_ticket:
    print("Entry allowed")

# or — at least one must be True
if age < 13 or age > 65:
    print("Discount eligible")

# not — inverts the boolean
if not is_banned:
    print("Not banned — can enter")

# Combining multiple
if age >= 18 and has_ticket and not is_banned:
    print("Welcome to the event!")
# Output: Welcome to the event!

blocks.text: Loops execute a block of code repeatedly. There are two fundamental loop types: **for loops** (iterate over a sequence) and **while loops** (repeat while a condition is true).

tabs.label: for Loop
tabs.code:
# Iterating over a range of numbers
for i in range(5):          # 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
    print(f"Count: {i}")

# Iterating over a list
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

# With index (enumerate)
for index, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(f"{index}: {fruit}")
# 0: apple
# 1: banana
# 2: cherry

# range(start, stop, step)
for n in range(0, 10, 2):   # 0, 2, 4, 6, 8
    print(n)

tabs.label: while Loop
tabs.code:
# while loop — runs as long as condition is True
countdown = 5
while countdown > 0:
    print(f"T-minus {countdown}")
    countdown -= 1           # countdown = countdown - 1
print("Liftoff!")

# Infinite loop with break
attempts = 0
while True:                  # runs forever...
    attempts += 1
    if attempts >= 3:        # ...until this break is hit
        print("Too many attempts")
        break
    print(f"Attempt {attempts}")

tabs.label: Loop Control

blocks.title: Loops inside loops (nested iteration)
blocks.code:
# Multiplication table
for i in range(1, 4):        # i: 1, 2, 3
    for j in range(1, 4):    # j: 1, 2, 3 (for each i)
        print(f"{i}×{j}={i*j}", end="  ")
    print()                  # newline after each row

# Output:
# 1×1=1  1×2=2  1×3=3  
# 2×1=2  2×2=4  2×3=6  
# 3×1=3  3×2=6  3×3=9  

# Searching in a 2D grid
grid = [[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6], [7, 8, 9]]
target = 5
found = False
for row in grid:
    for value in row:
        if value == target:
            found = True
            break
    if found:
        break
print(f"Found {target}: {found}")   # Found 5: True

blocks.title: Infinite Loops
blocks.text: A **while True** loop with no `break` condition will run forever, freezing your program. Always ensure your while loops have a guaranteed exit condition.

blocks.title: Pattern matching (Python 3.10+)
blocks.code:
# Python 3.10+ structural pattern matching
day = "Monday"

match day:
    case "Monday" | "Tuesday" | "Wednesday" | "Thursday" | "Friday":
        print("Weekday")
    case "Saturday" | "Sunday":
        print("Weekend")
    case _:                      # default case
        print("Unknown day")

# JavaScript switch
# switch (day) {
#   case 'Monday': ...; break;
#   default: ...
# }

points: if/elif/else selects code paths based on boolean conditions
for loops iterate over sequences or ranges; while loops run until a condition becomes false
break exits a loop immediately; continue skips the current iteration
Logical operators: and (both true), or (either true), not (inverts)
In JavaScript, use === instead of == to avoid type coercion surprises

questions.question:
What does this code print?
```
for i in range(5):
    if i == 3:
        break
    print(i)
```
questions.explanation: range(5) produces 0,1,2,3,4. When i equals 3, break exits the loop. So only 0, 1, 2 are printed (3 triggers the break before print runs).

questions.question: Which statement correctly combines two conditions in Python?
questions.explanation: Python uses the keyword 'and' (lowercase) to combine boolean conditions. '&&' is used in JavaScript/Java/C, not Python.
